one_line_takeaway: Across four reproduced LLM privacy attacks, dataset/document properties (domain, corpus size, PII repetition count) and model scale/architecture are consistently the dominant drivers of attack success, while retrieval configuration, prompt style, and search hyperparameters have comparatively marginal effects once other factors are fixed.
key_finding: Findings are attack-specific and dataset/factor-dependent rather than a single headline number. (1) S2MIA is highly sensitive to RAG dataset choice (ROC AUC 0.895 on SQuAD, 0.858 HotpotQA vs 0.662 NQ-Open and only 0.577 on TriviaQA, near chance) and corpus size (monotonic 0.472->0.690 ROC AUC from 25% to 100% of NQ-Open, contradicting the cited prior finding [10] that smaller corpora are easier to attack), while LLM choice, embedding model, and prompt style produced only narrow-band variation (ROC AUC ranges of roughly 0.55-0.68, 0.65-0.73, 0.57-0.62 respectively). (2) MBMIA achieves near-ceiling AUC (up to 1.0000, F1 0.9434 at m=15 masks) with Retrieval Recall pinned at 1.0 across all experiments, meaning attack variance stems from generator behavior (exact-match reconstruction fidelity) not retrieval failure; mask count m has the strongest effect (AUC 0.9032 at m=3 rising to 1.0000 at m>=15), while threshold gamma and retrieval depth K mainly reshape the operating point (F1) without changing ranking ability (AUC roughly flat). (3) AIA: model scale matters strongly within the Llama-2 family (Top-1 mean jumps from 37.3% at 7B params to 53.5% at 13B, a +16.2pp gain; Top-3 mean rises 23pp), and sparse/MoE architectures (DeepSeek-V3.2, Gemini-3) beat the dense Llama-2 by ~22.9pp average Top-1, but context-window size alone showed no clear additional effect among sparse models; exact-age accuracy is low (~20%) but rises to 69% under a +-5-year tolerance window, indicating approximate rather than precise leakage. (4) DEA on real (non-canary) Enron PII: mean bounded exposure across 11,827 scored templates is 2.16 bits (ceiling ~8.97 bits at N=501), with STRUCTURED_NAME (5.93 bits, 35.0% rank-1) and PHONE (4.08 bits, 22.9% rank-1) far more exposed than EMAIL (1.40 bits, 7.2% rank-1) due to EMAIL's larger format space; exposure rises monotonically with training-data repetition count (near-ceiling at >=16 repetitions), supporting deduplication as a mitigation; Dijkstra extraction over 30 high-exposure probes recovered the exact secret in 6/30 (20.0%) and a partial match in 16/30 (53.3%); model scale is the single dominant lever for extraction success (GPT-J-6B: 40.0% exact match vs GPT-Neo-2.7B/1.3B: 20.0%), while Dijkstra search depth/node budget (1,000-8,000 nodes) and candidate pool size (100-1,000) had negligible effect on exact-match rate, and adding synthetic prefix templates beyond ~5 per PII type actually reduced measured exposure (template dilution effect, 47% relative rank-1 decline from 3 to 15 templates).
core_contribution: This paper (UDST, Doha) is a systematization-and-ablation study of LLM privacy attacks, not a novel-attack paper. Its own contributions are: (1) a unified threat model/notation/taxonomy mapping Membership Inference Attacks (MIA), Attribute Inference Attacks (AIA), Data Extraction Attacks (DEA), and Backdoor Attacks (BA) onto shared LLM-lifecycle dimensions (origin stage, exploitation stage, access model, leakage granularity); (2) reproduction of representative attacks from prior work (S2MIA from Li et al., mask-based MIA/MBMIA from Liu et al., AIA from an unnamed cited attribute-inference framework, DEA following Carlini et al.'s exposure/Dijkstra-extraction method, and BadNets-style backdoor injection via the BackdoorLLM benchmark) inside one shared experimental framework; and (3) a large factor-by-factor empirical ablation (dataset, corpus size, membership ratio, model choice, embedding model, prompt style, model scale, retrieval stack, mask count, threshold, retrieval depth, Dijkstra search budget, candidate pool size, template count) across all four attack types, quantifying which system-design factors drive privacy risk.
threat_model: The paper explicitly separates four attack-specific threat models rather than proposing one new attack: (1) S2MIA/MBMIA: black-box query access to a deployed RAG system (retriever R + LLM M), no access to model weights, retriever internals, or database contents; adversary observes only textual outputs and infers document membership in the retrieval corpus D. (2) AIA: adversary has only access to public free-text authored by a user (no model access needed beyond prompting M) and constructs a prefix/suffix-wrapped prompt to elicit inferred attribute-value pairs from contextual cues alone. (3) DEA: black-box query access to an autoregressive LLM f_θ^M with next-token log-probability output; adversary knows only the approximate syntactic format of a target secret (e.g., email/phone), not whether it exists in training data D, and has no access to θ or gradients; extraction uses log-perplexity ranking plus a Dijkstra graph search over token continuations. (4) BA: adversary requires training/fine-tuning-time access for injection (data poisoning, weight poisoning, or PEFT manipulation) but only black-box access at activation/inference time; the study reproduces BadNets-style data-poisoning injection via the BackdoorLLM benchmark, trading off clean accuracy preservation against attack success rate.
limitations: The paper itself states: (a) single-seed experiments with no confidence intervals throughout the S2MIA ablation; (b) an untested balanced 50% membership ratio for S2MIA; (c) inconsistent perplexity behavior across models (gemma-2b-it exceeded 250,000 perplexity in one run); (d) most LLM-model and embedding-model configurations for S2MIA barely exceeded random-chance ROC AUC, undercutting claims of strong model-level effects; (e) an acknowledged corpus-source confound in the DEA dataset-scale ablation (Experiment 5.B) where the 50k/500k-document conditions drew from a different Enron partition (LLM-PBE/enron-email) than the model's actual training split, causing an anomalous Dijkstra collapse (3.3% exact match) that the authors attribute to reduced value repetition rather than a true scale effect; (f) DEA extraction success (20-40% exact match) is measured only on a small set of 30 high-exposure probes, limiting statistical power; (g) the domain-vs-retrieval-stack experiment for MBMIA (3.2.D) explicitly could not isolate domain effects from retriever/embedding effects with the available design; (h) AIA relies on synthetic conversations for reproducibility/ethics reasons, and some models (DeepSeek V3.2) required a workaround (system prompt injected as a preliminary chat turn) because they do not support system prompts, a methodological inconsistency across models being compared.

datasets_used: 525 synthetic conversations (AIA, from cited prior work [8])
suolyer/pile_enron (Enron email subset of The Pile) for DEA
LLM-PBE/enron-email (used in DEA dataset-scale ablation, noted as a confounding different partition)
BackdoorLLM benchmark data (for BA, per Li et al. [29])
quantitative_results: S2MIA ROC AUC by dataset: SQuAD 0.895, HotpotQA 0.858, NQ-Open 0.662, TriviaQA 0.577
S2MIA ROC AUC by membership ratio: 60% 0.799, 70% 0.769, 80% 0.656, 90% 0.692
S2MIA ROC AUC by LLM model: TinyLlama-1.1B 0.554, gemma-2b-it 0.588, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.2 0.561, Llama-2-7b-chat-hf 0.677, Qwen2-1.5B-Instruct 0.519
S2MIA ROC AUC by embedding model: contriever-msmarco 0.670, multi-qa-mpnet-base-dot-v1 0.658, all-MiniLM-L6-v2 0.734, all-mpnet-base-v2 0.674, bge-base-en-v1.5 0.650
S2MIA ROC AUC by prompt style: Regular 0.620, RTOC 0.624, Chain-of-Thought 0.570, CRISPE 0.616
MBMIA ROC AUC/F1 by model family (HealthcareMagic): Llama3 0.9980/0.9524, GPT-4o-mini 0.9934/0.8850, Phi-3 0.9104/0.7179, Mistral 0.7144/0.7711
MBMIA mask-count ablation ROC AUC: m=3 0.9032, m=5 0.9426, m=10 0.9932, m=15 1.0000, m=20 1.0000
AIA architecture comparison Top-1 mean: Llama2 (dense) 45.4%, DeepSeek-V3.2 (sparse DSA) 67.9%, Gemini-3 (sparse MoE) 68.7%
AIA age inference: exact-match ~20% for all models; best performer 69% under a +-5-year tolerance window

DEA bounded exposure by PII type (11,827 scored templates): EMAIL mean 1.40 bits / rank-1 7.2% (n=9,095), PHONE mean 4.08 bits / rank-1 22.9% (n=1,872), STRUCTURED_NAME mean 5.93 bits / rank-1 35.0% (n=860); overall mean 2.16 bits, rank-1 11.7%
DEA Dijkstra extraction over 30 high-exposure probes: exact match 6/30 (20.0%), partial match 16/30 (53.3%), no match 14/30 (46.7%)
DEA model-scale ablation: GPT-Neo 1.3B 14.0% rank-1 / 20.0% exact / 40.0% any-match; GPT-Neo 2.7B 15.5%/20.0%/40.0%; GPT-J 6B 21.3%/40.0%/56.7%
DEA dataset-scale ablation (GPT-Neo 2.7B): 1,957 docs 20.0% exact/40.0% any; 50,000 docs 3.3% exact/13.3% any (confounded corpus source); 500,000 docs 23.3% exact/50.0% any
DEA template-count ablation: 3 templates/type rank-1 18.7%, 5 templates 15.5%, 10 templates 11.3%, 15 templates 10.0% (47% relative decline, any-match roughly flat ~40-43%)

DEA combined-configuration experiment F: F4 (GPT-J-6B, depth 30, 10 templates) 36.7% exact/60.0% any-match; F5 (GPT-J-6B, 500k docs, 500 candidates, 15 templates) 30.0% exact/63.3% any-match, highest mean exposure 2.53 bits
baselines_compared: S2MIA baseline configuration reproduced from Li et al. [10] (Llama-2-7b-chat-hf, Contriever-msmarco, NQ-Open)
MBMIA reproduced from Liu et al. [11] mask-based mechanism (GPT-4o-mini, FAISS, BGE-small baseline)
AIA prompting method reproduced from cited prior work [8] (prefix/suffix contextual-inference framing)
DEA exposure metric and Dijkstra extraction algorithm reproduced from Carlini et al. [16], applied to real (non-canary) PII rather than planted canaries as in the original
BA reproduced via the BackdoorLLM benchmark (Li et al. [29]) using the BadNets data-poisoning algorithm (Gu et al. [32]) as baseline
